body:
ACCESS ADVISORY COMMITTEE 
M I N U T E S 
 
Wednesday, February 18, 2026, 5:00 pm 
Council Chamber, City Hall 
4949 Canada Way, Burnaby, BC 
 
PRESENT: 
Councillor Maita Santiago, Chair 
Councillor Richard T. Lee, Vice Chair 
Councillor Daniel Tetrault, Member 
Odette Brassard, Resident Representative 
Karim Damani, Resident Representative 
Mario Gregorio, Resident Representative 
Suzanne Norman, Resident Representative (participated electronically) 
Sonny Senghera, Resident Representative 
Anna Wijesinghe, Resident Representative 
ABSENT: 
Rod Bitz, Resident Representative 
STAFF: 
Amy Choh, Director Engineering Transportation 
Vishad Deeplaul, Senior Manager EDI (participated electronically) 
Peter Buono, Manager Recreation Facilities 
Kerry Teaves, Manager Recreation Services 
Andre Kolber, Manager Parks Operations (participated electronically) 
Noreen Ma, Manager Community Development 
Kyra Lubell, Planner 3 
Claire Shepansky, Planner 1 
Denise Chak, Administrative Officer 1 
 
1. 
CALL TO ORDER 
The Chair, Councillor Santiago, called the Committee meeting to order at 5:00 pm. 
 
2. 
TERRITORIAL ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
Chair Santiago respectfully acknowledged that the City of Burnaby is located on the 
unceded territories of the xʷməθkʷəy̓ əm (Musqueam), Sḵwx̱ wú7mesh (Squamish), 
səlilwətaɬ (Tsleil-Waututh), and kʷikʷəƛ̓ əm (Kwikwetlem) Peoples. Each Nation has 

Access Advisory Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 18, 2026 
 
 
2 
distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or partially encompass the 
city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host Nations whose ancestors 
have occupied and used these lands, including parts of present-day Burnaby, for 
thousands of years. 
 
3. 
ADOPTION OF AGENDA 
3.1 
Access Advisory Committee Agenda for February 18, 2026 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the agenda for the Access Advisory Committee meeting of February 18, 
2026 be adopted, as presented. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
4. 
ADOPTION OF MINUTES 
4.1 
Minutes of the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on October 1, 
2025 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the minutes of the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on October 1, 
2025, be now adopted. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
5. 
DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS 
5.1 
Delegation: Dementia Co-Creation Academy - Co-Creation through 
Recreation: Crafting Dementia Inclusive Communities 
Speaker: Farhad Farhangfar, Co-Founder, Dementia Co-Creation Academy 
Farhad Farhangfar, Co-Founder, Dementia Co-Creation Academy, appeared 
before the Committee to share information about their community-led initiative 
that started two years ago in Burnaby. The initiative aims to enhance social 
citizenship for people living with dementia and reduce stigma by bringing 
volunteers and people with lived experience together to participate in recreational 
activities, art projects and art-based therapeutic practices.  
The speaker noted that dementia inclusion is increasingly important as 
diagnoses rise globally and emphasized the value of fostering understanding and 
connection to build more inclusive communities. 

Access Advisory Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 18, 2026 
 
 
3 
6. 
ADMINISTRATIVE REPORTS 
No Administrative Reports were received. 
 
7. 
INFORMATION REPORTS 
7.1 
VERBAL REPORT: ACCESSIBILITY FEEDBACK FORM REPORTING - 
SEPTEMBER TO DECEMBER 2025 
Claire Shepansky, Planner 1, provided a verbal report on submissions received 
through the Accessibility Feedback Form for the period of September to 
December 2025. During this period, a total of 28 comments were received across 
the following five (5) categories: 
• Crosswalks and sidewalks: twelve (12) 
• Civic buildings and facilities: two (6) 
• Other: three (3) 
• Parks and outdoor space: three (3) 
• Adapted activities and programs: one (1) 
• Customer service: one (1) 
• Public washrooms: one (1) 
• Accessibility Plan: one (1) 
C. Shepansky further noted that, overall in 2025, 150 comments were received 
through the Accessibility Feedback Form, of which 130 were applicable and 20 
were deemed not applicable. Monthly trends indicated that periods with higher 
volumes of feedback aligned with promotional efforts undertaken by the 
Marketing and Communications team. 
In conclusion, the speaker outlined how feedback is reviewed and actioned and 
shared an example of work completed as a direct result of feedback received via 
the Accessibility Feedback Form. The Marketing and Communications team will 
initiate a second round of outreach to further promote the form.  
 
8. 
CORRESPONDENCE 
No items of correspondence were received. 
 
9. 
OTHER BUSINESS 
9.1 
Adapted Activities at City Facilities 

Access Advisory Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 18, 2026 
 
 
4 
Representative Senghera noted the Access Advisory Committee has been 
focusing on several key themes, including transportation infrastructure, 
education/awareness, and facilities. With respect to facilities, they inquired about 
the City’s adapted activities that allow youth and adults with disabilities to access 
programming at municipal facilities, specifically: 
• what activities are provided? 
• how do Burnaby’s offerings compare to other municipalities? 
• how has participation been trending? 
• how are these programs promoted and measured? 
• what policies are in place to remove barriers to access? 
• what plans are in place for future enhancements and community input? 
Staff advised that the City’s Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC) department 
offers a range of adapted and inclusive recreation opportunities, including 
one-to-one supports for preschoolers, children, and youth, inclusive camps, 
adaptive skating and swimming lessons (including ASL interpretation), 
sensory-adapted programs, and partnerships such as with the Canucks Autism 
Network. Additional offerings include social and targeted programming for 
pre-teens and adults, as well as drop-in recreation opportunities. 
For older adults, adapted and inclusive options include gentle fitness and 
balance classes, chair yoga, mobility programs, adapted AquaFit, active aging 
fitness programs, and some seniors’ bus trips with accessible transportation.  
Staff also highlighted facility and service supports, including accessible change 
rooms and washrooms, pool lifts, accessible pool entry points, staff inclusion 
training, and partnership programming with the BC Provincial School for the 
Deaf. 
In conclusion, staff noted that Burnaby’s adapted recreation offerings are high 
compared to other municipalities, with continued demand for one-to-one 
supports. Adapted programs are promoted through the seasonal activity guide, 
the City’s website, and social media channels. 
 
9.2 
Tactile Markings at Brentwood Town Centre 
Representative Damani inquired whether the tactile markings adjacent to the 
floating bus stop and bike lane at Brentwood Town Centre have been changed or 
removed, noting concerns that they may have been incorrectly directing uses and 
presenting potential safety issues. 

Access Advisory Committee Meeting 
Minutes February 18, 2026 
 
 
5 
Staff advised the design of the tactile markings at Brentwood Town Centre 
follows the TransLink design guide for bus stops adjacent to cycling 
infrastructure. The guide was developed by TransLink and the BC Ministry of 
Transportation and Transit in 2024. 
With the project now complete, the Transportation team is arranging a site visit to 
seek further feedback from the Access Advisory Committee, the Canadian 
National Institute for the Blind (CNIB), and TransLink on the installed measures.  
 
9.3 
Creating Opportunities to Alleviate Loneliness and Foster Social 
Connections 
Representative Gregorio acknowledged the collective role of the City, 
policymakers, community partners, and academia in advancing accessible 
facilities and programs that strengthen social connections among residents. They 
also shared an example illustrating that participation in weekly ukulele classes 
supported functional improvement and increased community engagement for an 
individual living with dementia, highlighting the value of community arts programs 
in helping participants remain connected. 
 
10. 
ADJOURNMENT 
By unanimous consent and without objection, the Committee meeting adjourned at 6:25 
pm. 
 
 
 
 
 
Councillor Maita Santiago 
CHAIR 
 
Denise Chak 
ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER

---

Purpose: To seek feedback from Access Advisory Committee members on topics for a disability‑focused awareness campaign. Staff presentation by Frances Tang-Graham, Acting Director, Communications and Marketing

Burnaby Disability 
Awareness Campaign
Marketing & Communications Campaign Proposal
AAC April 15, 2026

Accessibility Plan
•
Action 3.1: Create and share disability focused awareness 
programming that highlights the voices of people with disabilities. 
•
Received funding from SPARC BC grant for honorariums.

Campaign Goals
•
Reduce ableism in our community, especially in and around City-run 
facilities, events, and services.
•
Challenge stereotypes and address misconceptions about disability 
and access.
•
Promote how to contact City staff about accessibility concerns and 
accommodations at our facilities, events, and in our services.

Campaign Details 
Material to produce: 
•
3-4 educational social media reels (~1 minute) featuring people in our 
community with disabilities sharing their life experiences- how long they have 
lived with disability, how it has impacted their lives, the challenges and barriers 
they overcome daily and how accessibility changes improve them.
•
Supporting infographics highlighting the key messages and important data 
behind each video.
•
Digital screens in civic facilities (e.g. libraries, @ city events) addressing 
common challenges (e.g. invisible disability, sensory overstimulation).
•
Improvements to City of Burnaby website addressing accessibility in City 
spaces and directing concerns to the Accessibility Feedback Form. 

Campaign Details 
In-market dates:
•
Start May 25 (beginning of Access Ability Week)
•
Digital spending ends Aug 31 (end of summer/peak usage of City facilities and 
events)
•
Organic/unpaid promotion continues as evergreen content
Budget:
•
Honoraria for participants $500
•
Digital promotion/spending $500
•
Total: $1,000

Campaign Strategy 
•
Use a storytelling approach that highlights real people’s lived 
experiences.
•
Focus on fewer, high-quality communications that will last and make 
an impression- too many can be tuned out! 
•
Engage with participatory, interactive prompts, e.g. “spot the 
barrier”
•
Focus on digital communications- COB’s largest audience and the 
most cost-effective way to spend our advertising budget.

Campaign Details 
Workback:
•
Apr 15 – AA committee meeting to approve topics
•
Apr 21 – Call for participants for videos (honoraria provided) – ask committee
•
May 15 – Participants selected. Each provided with prompts/guide questions 
for their voiceover. 
•
May 29 – Deadline for participants to provide content (photos, video) 
•
Jun 5 – 1st proofs of social content 
•
Jun 12 – Final proofs of social content
•
Jun 19 – Supporting infographics created for review
•
Jul 6 – Campaign launch

Campaign Topics
•
Committee to advise on priorities for subjects covered.
•
For max impact, suggest we choose 3 or 4 and concentrate our 
messaging- public attention is short!
•
To make an impression, our communications should be focused and 
brief- under 1 minute, and ideally around 30 seconds.
•
Focus on city spaces and services- our staff resources need to be 
allocated to COB-related projects.

Campaign Topics
Think about sparking engagement and opportunities to participate! 
What stories should we highlight in this campaign? 
» For example: 
•
Types of disability & invisible disabilities
•
Guide dog handler rights and guide dog etiquette
•
Sensory overstimulation and low-sensory areas
•
Barriers beyond physical access: attitudinal, misconceptions, assumptions, 
technology
•
Language and how we speak about disability

---

Purpose: To provide Committee with information on the completed Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment. Staff presentation by Erin Rennie, Planner 3.

Burnaby 2050 
Official 
Community Plan
+
Social Infrastructure 
Needs Assessment
Access Advisory Committee
April 15, 2026

Agenda
1. What is Burnaby 2050? 
• Growth and Change
• OCP Overview
• Policy Framework
• Plan Highlights 
2. Social Infrastructure Needs 
Assessment
• Public Schools Profile 
• Community Resource Centres
Profile 
• Next Steps 

Burnaby 2050
•
Burnaby’s new Official Community Plan (OCP) 
•
A long-term vision for the community. 
•
Provides the strategic direction, framework and 
guidance to meet the anticipated needs of the 
community while ensuring Burnaby remains livable.
•
Comprehensive policy plan covering all planning topics 
and a long-range land use plan to guide development 
decisions. 
•
2022-2025: robust multi-phase engagement process
•
Adopted December 2025

Growth and Change
Growth Projections 
Forecasted Numbers 
2021
2030
2040
2050
Growth 
(2021-
2050)
Population 
261,810
311,510
361,630
408,150
+55.9%
Housing 
Units
106,170
128,330
151,860
174,060
+63.9%
Jobs 
160,330
185,490
209,940
231,820
+44.6%
Key drivers of change 
•
A growing and aging 
population 
•
Housing affordability and 
housing options 
•
Climate change 
•
A changing economy
•
Immigration
•
Aging infrastructure 
•
Provincial housing policy 

OCP Overview 
Vision
“Burnaby is a green and inclusive city with safe and connected neighbourhoods and 
a vibrant economy where people want to live, work and play for generations to come.”
Three 
Lenses

OCP Overview 
The Plan at a Glance 
Vision
Implementation
Land Use Framework
Policy Framework

OCP Overview
Land Use Framework 
The Land Use Framework guides development to meet 
Burnaby’s anticipated residential, employment, 
environmental and community needs. Notably, the Land 
Use Framework uses a parcel-based Land Use Map. 

Policy Framework 
OCP Goals 
Goal 1: Foster complete communities that are sustainable, equitable and accessible to everyone 
Goal 2: Create inclusive public spaces that are accessible and respond to everyday needs 
Goal 3: Support a diverse and thriving economy 
Goal 4: Create and maintain efficient and well-connected transportation and infrastructure networks 
Goal 5: Lead in climate action and protect, enhance and restore the natural environment 

Plan Highlights
Tenant 
protection
Transit-oriented 
development
Housing choices
Transportation 
connections
New 
neighbourhood 
centres
Amenities to 
support growth
Welcoming 
public spaces
Environmental 
protection
…and more
Actions for 
reconciliation
Support for small 
businesses

Social Infrastructure Needs 
Assessment

What is Social Infrastructure (SI) for Burnaby
Social infrastructure are 
facilities and spaces that 
are used for social and 
community services, 
activities and events 
delivered by the City, non-
profits, private sector and 
other community operators 
for groups and individuals. 
Outcomes:
•
Health and well-being
•
Social connection and 
belonging
•
Daily needs 
•
Life-long learning

Type of Social Infrastructure
• Child care centres
• Community Resource Centres (CRCs)
• Public libraries
• Arts and culture facilities *
• Recreation facilities *
• Parks * 
• Public schools
• Healthcare facilities
• Commercial & retail spaces
*These SI types under the Parks, Recreation and Cultural portfolio 
will be included in Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and 
Culture Plan. 

Project Background & Methodology
• Project rationale: 
•
SI should be planned to keep pace with growth
•
Inform the OCP, other future City strategies
• Data Gathering, including:
• Best Practice Review
• Mapping
• Engagement Data with Community Partners 
• Identification of needs and gaps across Burnaby based on:
• Infrastructure
• Population
• Daily Needs 
• Transportation
• SI findings were put into profiles that tried to answer: 
•
Priorities per quadrant and Community Plan Area
•
Amount of floor space is required to meet the needs
•
How to meet the needs

Engagement Findings
Challenges 
•
Affordability 
•
Suitability 
•
Need for more 
program space and 
rental space
Opportunities 
• Locate new SI in places 
convenient to transit
• Foster co-location 
• More passive spaces for 
informal gatherings and social 
connections
Design Considerations 
• Multi-purpose spaces 
• Space for storage to support 
co-location 
• Informal spaces for gathering 
that do not require bookings 
(e.g., picnic tables, lobbies) 
• Dedicated spaces for youth 
within a facility 
• Accessible design for public 
washrooms and 
accommodating mobility 
devices 

Accessibility Considerations in SINA 
Engagement 
Consultation with local accessibility/disability-
focused organizations including BACI, 
posAbilities, BC Centre for Abilities. 
Findings and Recommendations 
•
Locating services and amenities near 
SkyTrain and frequent transit 
•
Facility design considerations such as 
accessible washrooms and 
accommodation for wheelchairs and 
mobility devices 

Summary of Quadrant Prioritization

Profile Extract: Community Resource Centres
Strategic Recommendations
In conjunction with the Social Sustainability Strategy and Burnaby 2050 OCP, 
the City may consider: 
1.
Explore creating a service provision target for CRCs. 
2.
Encourage and support on-site CRCs for City-owned facilities delivered 
through Community Benefit Bonus. 
3.
Explore and confirm ideal minimum size for CRCs. 
4.
Create design standards for CRCs. 
5.
Conduct research on co-location facility models and administration 
models for CRCs. 
6.
Explore capacity-building opportunities or partnerships with non-profits 
to build literacy, knowledge and strategic planning for organizational 
facility/space needs. 

Next Steps – Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment
OCP Implementation
•
Develop OCP Implementation Action Plan 
(implementation actions & timeframe, monitoring 
framework, reporting plan)
•
5 year updates
Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment: 
•
Inform the City of Burnaby’s amenity framework 
and implementation strategy (Community Amenity 
Strategy).
•
Internal staff resource for planning, reporting etc.

Thank you

Purpose: To provide the Committee with an update on submissions received through the Accessibility Feedback Form from January to March 2026.

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby disability awareness campaign and accessibility updates
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to meet on April 15, 2026, to give feedback on a proposed disability-focused awareness campaign planned to launch during National AccessAbility Week (starting May 25). The committee will also receive updates on accessibility feedback submissions, a completed social infrastructure study, and ongoing projects like the Brentwood Town Centre accessibility pilot.
briefs.action_required: Watch for campaign launch May 25 — it may affect how customers interact with your accessible City facilities.

key_dates.label: Burnaby disability awareness campaign — proposed launch date
Burnaby AccessAbility Week campaign — digital promotion period ends
Burnaby Access Advisory Committee meeting — accessibility updates
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — call for video participants opens
National AccessAbility Week — Burnaby campaign in-market start

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby Access Advisory Committee minutes from February 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on accepting the minutes from the Access Advisory Committee meeting held on February 18, 2026. At that meeting, the committee reviewed accessibility feedback from residents, heard about dementia-inclusive recreation programs, and discussed adapted activities at city facilities and tactile safety markings near Brentwood Town Centre.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is near Brentwood Town Centre — a site review of tactile safety markings is planned.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign Seeks Community Input on Topics
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to consider a proposed disability-focused awareness campaign planned to launch in summer 2026. The campaign would produce short social media videos, infographics, and digital screen content featuring real community members with disabilities — with a total budget of $1,000 and a target launch date of July 6, 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you or your staff work near City facilities or events, watch for this campaign — it may affect accessibility expectations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — call for video participants opens
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — participant selection deadline
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — content submission deadline
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — planned launch date
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital ad spending ends

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment for Long-Term Community Growth
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a completed Social Infrastructure Needs Assessment (SINA) — a study that maps out what community spaces and services (like childcare centres, schools, and community resource centres) the city will need as its population grows by nearly 56% by 2050. The findings are expected to shape how the city plans and funds new community facilities, and could influence rules around developer contributions and where new amenities get built.
briefs.action_required: Watch for follow-up council decisions on the Community Amenity Strategy — it could affect development contributions.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Accessibility Feedback Form Update for Q1 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Accessibility Committee will consider a verbal report covering feedback submitted through the City's Accessibility Feedback Form between January and March 2026. This is an informational update — no bylaw or rule change is being proposed at this time — but it may shape future accessibility decisions that could affect businesses and public spaces in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Review the meeting agenda if accessibility compliance affects your business or property.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign Launching National AccessAbility Week
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to review and provide feedback on a proposed disability awareness campaign at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The campaign would run short videos and social media content featuring real community members with disabilities, aiming to reduce ableism and promote accessibility resources at City facilities and events.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's decision — the campaign launches May 25, 2026, during National AccessAbility Week.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — call for video participants opens
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — National AccessAbility Week launch date
Burnaby Disability Awareness Campaign — digital promotion end date

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby disability awareness campaign launching May 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to review and provide feedback on a proposed disability-focused awareness campaign at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The campaign would run from late May through August 2026, using short social media videos and digital displays at city facilities to challenge stereotypes about disability and promote accessibility resources — with a total budget of $1,000.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's decision — the campaign launches May 25, 2026 at city facilities and online.

key_dates.label: Burnaby disability awareness campaign — Access Advisory Committee feedback meeting
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — National AccessAbility Week launch date
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — digital promotion period ends
Burnaby disability awareness campaign — full public launch date

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Town Centre Accessibility Pilot Project Review
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to discuss the Brentwood Town Centre Accessibility Pilot Project at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The committee will also consider feedback on tactile markings (textured ground surfaces that guide people with vision loss) near the floating bus stop and bike lane, following a planned site visit with accessibility groups including the CNIB and TransLink.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the committee's findings — changes to Brentwood Town Centre access routes could affect your customers or deliveries.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Access Advisory Committee meeting — Brentwood accessibility pilot discussed
Burnaby disability awareness campaign launch — National AccessAbility Week

briefs.title: Council to consider accessibility measures for Burnaby's 2026 General Local Election
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Access Advisory Committee is set to discuss accessibility measures for the 2026 General Local Election at its April 15, 2026 meeting. The committee will consider what steps the City should take to make voting and election participation easier for people with disabilities — no final decision has been made yet.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it may affect how accessible your polling location or election services are.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Access Advisory Committee meeting — 2026 election accessibility measures discussed
